segments.title: Fast-firing guns against torpedo boats
segments.summary: The Royal Navy's basic answer to an E-boat or other torpedo boat was rapid-firing gunfire.
segments.body: Clarke frames the problem as a familiar one: how to stop a fast torpedo boat before it can make its attack. His compact answer is fast-firing guns, a reminder that close-range coastal defence depended on hitting small, agile targets quickly rather than trying to match their torpedo tactics.

evidence.note: The summary identifies fast-firing guns as the answer to torpedo boats and small-boat attacks.

segments.title: Reaction speed against E-boat ambushes
segments.summary: E-boats were especially dangerous where traffic, darkness, or radar deception shortened the defender's warning time.
segments.body: An ambush craft gains its advantage when it can appear in a crowded sea lane, at night, or under conditions that compromise early radar. Clarke therefore stresses speed of reaction: the defending force must respond quickly enough to drive the attacker off or destroy it before the attack develops.

evidence.note: The transcript explains how limited visibility, crowded lanes, and early-radar spoofing demand rapid defensive reaction.

segments.title: Coastal gunboats built to stop E-boats
segments.summary: Steam Gun Boats, Fairmile Ds, and heavily armed small escorts concentrated rapid fire for close E-boat engagements.
segments.body: Clarke contrasts the required counter-force with the older Insect-class gunboats and points instead to small craft able to deploy quickly with enough firepower to press an attacker away. He cites Steam Gun Boats and Fairmile Ds as examples. The extra guns on such escorts could look like anti-aircraft armament, but the point here was rapid fire against an E-boat that came within range.

evidence.note: The transcript names Steam Gun Boats and Fairmile Ds, and explains that rapid-firing weapons on small escorts could be intended for E-boat defence.
